name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ كَانَ مِيقَـٰتًا (١٧)
```

```english
Indeed the Day of Judgement is the tryst, (17)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْوَاجًا (١٨)
```

```english
the day the Trumpet will be blown, and you will come in groups, (18)
```

```arabic
وَفُتِحَتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ أَبْوَٰبًا (١٩)
```

```english
and the sky will be opened and become gates, (19)
```

```arabic
وَسُيِّرَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ فَكَانَتْ سَرَابًا (٢٠)
```

```english
and the mountains will be set moving and become a mirage. (20)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كَانَتْ مِرْصَادًا (٢١)
```

```english
Indeed hell is in ambush, (21)
```

```arabic
لِّلطَّـٰغِينَ مَـَٔابًا (٢٢)
```

```english
a resort for the rebels, (22)
```

```arabic
لَّـٰبِثِينَ فِيهَآ أَحْقَابًا (٢٣)
```

```english
to reside therein for ages, (23)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا بَرْدًا وَلَا شَرَابًا (٢٤)
```

```english
tasting in it neither any coolness nor drink, (24)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا حَمِيمًا وَغَسَّاقًا (٢٥)
```

```english
except boiling water and pus, (25)
```

```arabic
جَزَآءً وِفَاقًا (٢٦)
```

```english
a fitting requital. (26)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ حِسَابًا (٢٧)
```

```english
Indeed they did not expect any reckoning, (27)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كِذَّابًا (٢٨)
```

```english
and they denied Our signs mendaciously, (28)
```

```arabic
وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ كِتَـٰبًا (٢٩)
```

```english
and We have figured everything in a Book. (29)
```

```arabic
فَذُوقُوا۟ فَلَن نَّزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذَابًا (٣٠)
```

```english
So [now] taste! We shall increase you in nothing but punishment! (30)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفَازًا (٣١)
```

```english
Indeed a triumph awaits the Godwary: (31)
```

```arabic
حَدَآئِقَ وَأَعْنَـٰبًا (٣٢)
```

```english
gardens and vineyards, (32)
```

```arabic
وَكَوَاعِبَ أَتْرَابًا (٣٣)
```

```english
and buxom maidens of a like age, (33)
```

```arabic
وَكَأْسًا دِهَاقًا (٣٤)
```

```english
and brimming cups. (34)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا كِذَّٰبًا (٣٥)
```

```english
Therein they shall hear neither vain talk nor lies (35)
```

```arabic
جَزَآءً مِّن رَّبِّكَ عَطَآءً حِسَابًا (٣٦)
```

```english
—a reward and a sufficing bounty from your Lord, (36)
```

```arabic
رَّبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا (٣٧)
```

```english
the All-beneficent, the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. They will not be able to address Him (37)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلرُّوحُ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ صَفًّا ۖ لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا (٣٨)
```

```english
on the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array: none shall speak except someone who is permitted by the All-beneficent and says what is rightful. (38)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ مَـَٔابًا (٣٩)
```

```english
That day is true for certain. So let anyone who wishes take resort with his Lord. (39)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنذَرْنَـٰكُمْ عَذَابًا قَرِيبًا يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ ٱلْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْكَافِرُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ تُرَٰبًۢا (٤٠)
```

```english
Indeed We have warned you of a punishment near at hand—the day when a person will observe what his hands have sent ahead and the faithless one will say, ‘I wish I were dust!’ (40)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses describe the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل), which was previously mentioned in general terms by His saying: "No indeed! Soon they will know!" (كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ). It then describes what will occur therein for the rebellious (الطاغين) and the God-conscious (المتقين), concluding with a word of warning that serves as a conclusion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ كانَ مِيقاتاً** — **Indeed the Day of Distinction is an appointed time (17).** Al-Tabarsi said in *al-Majma‘*: "The appointed time (الميقات) is the ultimate measure set for the occurrence of a matter; it is derived from 'time' (الوقت), just as 'the promised time' (الميعاد) is from 'promise' (الوعد) and 'measure' (المقدار) is from 'power/decree' (القدر)."

This is the commencement of the description of what is contained within the Great News (النبأ العظيم), the occurrence of which He announced and with which He threatened them in His saying: "No indeed! Soon they will know!" (كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ). He then established the proof for it by His saying: "Did We not make the earth a resting place..." (أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ مِهاداً) etc. He named it the Day of Distinction (يوم الفصل) — *the day when judgment is decided between people* — to point out that it is a day when the decree is settled between people, and every group receives what it deserves for its deeds. Thus, it is an appointed time (ميقات) and a set limit for the distinction of judgment between them. The use of the past tense "was" (كانَ) indicates its fixity and determination in the Divine Knowledge (العلم الإلهي) based on what the aforementioned proof dictates; for this reason, He emphasized the sentence with "Indeed" (إنّ).

The meaning is: The day of the distinction of judgment, whose news is a great news, was in the knowledge of Allah when He created the heavens and the earth and decreed within them the current system as a set limit to which this world ends. For the Exalted knew that this creation (النشأة) which He initiated would not be complete except by ending in a day when judgment is distinguished between them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْواجاً** — **the day the Trumpet is blown, and you come in groups (18).** The discussion regarding the meaning of the blowing of the Trumpet (نفخ الصور) has been mentioned repeatedly. "Groups" (الأفواج) is the plural of *fawj* (فوج), which refers to a passing and hurrying company, according to what al-Raghib mentioned.

In His saying: "and you come in groups" (فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْواجاً), there is a continuation of the previous direct address (الخطاب) which was turned toward them to fulfill the requirement of the threat contained in His saying: "No indeed! Soon they will know!" (كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ). It is as if the verse is looking toward His saying, the Exalted: "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam" (يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ) [Al-Isra: 71].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَفُتِحَتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ أَبْواباً** — **and the sky is opened and becomes gates (19),** such that the world of man becomes connected to the world of the angels (الملائكة).

It has been said that the implied meaning is "it becomes possessed of gates," and it has been said that paths are formed within it where none existed before. Neither of these two views is free from arbitrariness, so let one reflect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً** — **and the mountains are set moving and become a mirage (20).** A mirage (السراب) is the illusory appearance of water shimmering in the wilderness; it is applied metaphorically to everything that is imagined to have a reality (حقيقة) when it has none.

Perhaps the intended meaning of "mirage" in the verse is this second meaning.

The explanation for this is that the setting of mountains in motion and their crushing naturally ends in the scattering of their parts and the vanishing of their form, as occurs in various places in the speech of the Exalted when describing the earthquake of the Hour (زلزلة الساعة) and its effects, where He says: "and the mountains are set moving" (وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً) [At-Tur: 10], and says: "and the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed with a single blow" (وَحُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً) [Al-Haqqah: 14], and says: "and the mountains become a shifting heap of sand" (وَكانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلاً) [Al-Muzzammil: 14], and says: "and the mountains become like carded wool" (وَتَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ) [Al-Qari'ah: 5], and says: "and the mountains are crumbled into pieces" (وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا) [Al-Waqi'ah: 5], and says: "and when the mountains are scattered" (وَإِذَا الْجِبالُ نُسِفَتْ) [Al-Mursalat: 10].

Thus, the moving of the mountains and their crushing ends in their being crumbled, scattered, and becoming a shifting heap of sand and like carded wool, as Allah the Exalted mentioned. As for their becoming a mirage in the sense of imagined shimmering water, there is no direct correlation between the moving (التسيير) and the mirage in this sense.

Rather, their moving ends in their non-existence and the nullification of their being (كينونتها) and their reality (حقيقتها) in terms of being a mountain. The firm mountains that were seen as realities of powerful existence (ذوات كينونة قوية) which storms could not move, are transformed by the moving into a false mirage (سراباً باطلاً) with no reality. Similar to this in His speech is His saying regarding peoples He destroyed and whose remnants He cut off: "and We made them [bywords of] tales" (فَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ) [Saba: 19], and His saying: "So We made them follow one another [to perdition] and We made them [bywords of] tales" (فَأَتْبَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ بَعْضاً وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ) [Al-Mu'minun: 44], and His saying regarding idols: "They are but names which you have named—you and your fathers" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ) [An-Najm: 23].

Thus, the verse is in a way like His saying: "And you see the mountains, which you suppose to be stationary, while they drift like clouds" (وَتَرَى الْجِبالُ تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ) [An-Naml: 88] — assuming it refers to the description of the earthquake of the Hour.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كانَتْ مِرْصاداً** — **Indeed hell is an ambush (21).** Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Observation (الرصد) is the preparation for watching... and the place of observation (المرصد) is the location of watching; the Exalted said: 'and lie in wait for them at every lookout' (وَاقْعُدُوا لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ). The term *mirsad* (المرصاد) is similar, but it is used for a place specifically designated for observation. The Exalted said: 'Indeed hell is an ambush' (إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ كانَتْ مِرْصاداً) as a warning that it is the passage for people. Based on this is His saying: 'There is none of you but will come to it' (وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها)."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِلطَّاغِينَ مَآباً** — **a resort for the rebels (22).** The rebels (الطاغون) are those characterized by rebellion (الطغيان) — *the act of exceeding the proper bounds*. The resort (المآب) is a noun of place derived from *awb* (الأوب), meaning "return." The significance in counting it as a resort for the rebels is that they prepared it as a dwelling for themselves while they were in the world; then, when they are severed from the world, they return (آبوا) to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لابِثِينَ فِيها أَحْقاباً** — **to reside therein for ages (23).** Ages (الأحقاب) refers to many periods and long epochs without specific limitation.

It is a plural regarding whose singular form scholars have differed. It was said the singular is *huqb* (حقب) with a *damma* then *sukun*, or with two *dammas*; this occurs in His saying: "or I will go on for ages" (أَوْ أَمْضِيَ حُقُباً) [Al-Kahf: 60]. It was also said the singular is *haqb* (حقب) with a *fatha* then *sukun*, and the singular of *al-huqub* is *huqbah* (حقبة) with a *kasra* then *sukun*. Al-Raghib said: "The truth is that *al-huqbah* is an indefinite period of time."

Some defined the *huqb* as eighty years or eighty-odd years; others added that each year consists of three hundred and sixty days, each day equaling a thousand years. It was narrated from some that a *huqb* is forty years, and from others that it is seventy thousand years, among other views. There is no evidence from the Book indicating any of these definitions, nor has anything been established from the language regarding them.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that the "rebels" refers to the obstinate among the disbelievers (الكفار); this is supported by His saying at the end: "Indeed they did not expect any reckoning, and they denied Our signs with a total denial" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا لا يَرْجُونَ حِساباً وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا كِذَّاباً).

They have interpreted "ages" (أَحْقاباً) in the verse as one age after another; thus, the meaning is that the rebels reside in Hell for age after age without limit or end. Therefore, the verse does not contradict what the Quran explicitly states regarding the eternity (خلود) of the disbelievers in the Fire.

It was also said that His saying: "they shall not taste therein..." (لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيها) etc., is an attribute (صفة) of "ages" (أَحْقاباً). The meaning would be: residing therein for ages characterized by this attribute — that they taste therein neither coolness nor drink except boiling water and pus — and then they will be in a different state for eternity.

This is a fine interpretation if the context (السياق) supported it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيها بَرْداً وَلا شَراباً** — **They shall not taste therein any coolness or drink (24).** The apparent contrast between "coolness" (البرد) and "drink" (الشراب) suggests that "coolness" refers to anything that provides cooling other than drink, such as shade in which one finds rest. "Tasting" (الذوق) here refers to any form of attaining or touching.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا حَمِيماً وَغَسَّاقاً** — **except boiling water and pus (25).** Boiling water (الحميم) is water that is intensely hot, and pus (الغساق) is the discharge of the people of the Fire.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **جَزاءً وِفاقاً** — **a fitting reward (26)** — up to His saying — **كِتاباً** — **in a book (29).** The verbal noun (المصدر) here carries the meaning of the active participle (اسم الفاعل); the meaning is that they are rewarded with a reward that accords (موافقاً) with what they did. Alternatively, a possessive is implied: "a reward possessed of accord," or the use of "accord" (الوفاق) for the reward is for emphasis, like saying "Zayd is justice."

His saying: "Indeed they did not expect any reckoning, and they denied Our signs with a total denial" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا لا يَرْجُونَ حِساباً وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا كِذَّاباً) — meaning a strange denial upon which they persisted — is an explanation clarifying the accordance of their reward with their deed. This is because they did not expect the reckoning on the Day of Distinction, so they despaired of the Hereafter and denied the signs pointing to it. Thus, they rejected monotheism (التوحيد) and prophethood (النبوة) and exceeded the bounds of servitude (العبودية) in their actions. They forgot Allah, so He forgot them and forbade them the felicity of the Abode of the Hereafter. Nothing remained for them but wretchedness; they find therein only what they loathe and face only what causes them torment, which is His saying: "So taste! We shall not increase you in anything but punishment" (فَذُوقُوا فَلَنْ نَزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذاباً).

In the verse — namely His saying: "a fitting reward" (جَزاءً وِفاقاً) — there is an indication of the perfect correspondence between the reward and the deed. For man, through his deed, desires nothing but the reward that corresponds to it, and being clothed in the reward is, in reality, being clothed in the deed. The Exalted said: "O you who disbelieve! Do not make excuses today. You are only being rewarded for what you used to do" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ) [At-Tahrim: 7].

His saying: "and We have figured everything in a book" (وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ كِتاباً) — meaning everything, including deeds, We have recorded and clarified in a book of great status. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying: "and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam" (وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ) [Ya-Sin: 12].

Or the meaning is: everything We have preserved while it is written — i.e., in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) or in the scrolls of deeds. It is also permissible that "figuring" (الإحصاء) carries the meaning of writing, or "book" (الكتاب) carries the meaning of figuring, as figuring and writing share the meaning of recording (الضبط). The meaning would be: everything We have figured with a figuring, or everything We have written with a writing.

The verse, in any case, completes the previous explanation. The meaning is: the reward accords with their deeds because they were in such-and-such a state, and We have preserved those deeds against them, so We rewarded them for them with a fitting reward.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذُوقُوا فَلَنْ نَزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذاباً** — **So taste! We shall not increase you in anything but punishment (30).** This is a derivation from the preceding detail of their punishment, aimed at making them despair of hoping for any salvation from wretchedness or any comfort they might attain.

The shift (الالتفات) to addressing them with "So taste!" (فَذُوقُوا) presupposes their presence so they may be addressed with rebuke and reprimand directly.

The meaning of His saying: "We shall not increase you in anything but punishment" (فَلَنْ نَزِيدَكُمْ إِلَّا عَذاباً) is that what you taste after a punishment you have already tasted is another punishment; thus, it is punishment after punishment and punishment upon punishment. New punishment will continually be added to your old punishment, so despair of attaining anything of what you seek and love.

The verse is not devoid of an apparent indication that the meaning of His saying: "residing therein for ages" (لابِثِينَ فِيها أَحْقاباً) is eternity (الخلود) without cessation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفازاً** — **Indeed for the God-conscious there is a triumph (31)** — up to His saying — **كِذَّاباً** — **denial (35).** Triumph (الفوز) is the attaining of good along with the achievement of safety — as al-Raghib said. Thus, it contains the meaning of salvation and deliverance from evil and the acquisition of good. *Mafaz* (المفاز) is either a mim-infinitve or a noun of place derived from triumph; the verse allows for both possibilities.

His saying: "gardens and vineyards" (حَدائِقَ وَأَعْناباً) — *Hada'iq* (الحدائق) is the plural of *hadiqah*, which is an enclosed garden. *A'nab* (الأعناب) is the plural of *inab*, which is the fruit of the vine, and sometimes it is applied to the tree itself.

His saying: "and buxom maidens" (وَكَواعِبَ) — *Kawa'ib* (كواعب) is the plural of *ka'ib*, which is a young woman whose breasts have become rounded and slightly elevated. *Atrab* (أتراب) is the plural of *tirb*, which refers to those of equal age and likeness to others in delights.

His saying: "and a brimming cup" (وَكَأْساً دِهاقاً) — meaning filled with drink; it is a verbal noun carrying the meaning of the active participle.

His saying: "They shall not hear therein any vain talk or denial" (لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا كِذَّاباً) — meaning they shall not hear in Paradise any vain speech (لغواً) from which no desired effect follows, nor any denial (تكذيباً) of one another in what they say. Their speech is truth (حق) that has its desired effect and is a veracity (صدق) corresponding to reality.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **جَزاءً مِنْ رَبِّكَ عَطاءً حِساباً** — **a reward from your Lord, a calculated gift (36).** That is, He did for the God-conscious what He did as a reward from your Lord, a gift that is calculated. His saying "a reward" (جَزاءً) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال), as is "a gift" (عَطاءً). "Calculated" (حِساباً) carries the meaning of the passive participle (اسم المفعول) as an attribute of "gift." It is also possible that "gift" is a specification (تمييز) or an absolute object (مفعول مطلق).

It has been said: The attribution of the reward to "the Lord" (الرب) while being annexed to the pronoun of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is an honor for him. The reward of the rebels was not attributed to Him, the Exalted, out of transcendence (تنزهاً) on His part; for no evil touches them except from themselves. The Exalted said: "That is for what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not unjust to [His] servants" (ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ) [Al-Anfal: 51].

The occurrence of the word "reckoning/calculated" (الحساب) at the end of the reward for both the rebels and the God-conscious is to affirm what is alluded to by the Day of Distinction mentioned at the beginning of the discourse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا الرَّحْمنِ** — **the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-beneficent (37).** This is an explanation of His saying: "your Lord" (رَبِّكَ). It is intended to show that His Lordship (الربوبية) is general to everything, and that the Lord whom the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) takes as a Lord and to whom he calls is the Lord of everything — not as the polytheists used to say, that for every group of beings there is a lord and Allah is the Lord of lords, or as some of them said, that He is the Lord of the heaven.

In describing the Lord as "the All-beneficent" (الرحمن) — an intensive form of mercy (الرحمة) — there is an indication of the vastness of His mercy and that it is a mark of Lordship from which nothing is deprived unless it prevents itself through its own deficiency and ill-choice. It is part of the wretchedness of these rebels that they deprived themselves of it by exiting the state of servitude.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطاباً يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً** — **they will not be able to address Him. On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array, they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent allows, and who says what is right (37-38).** The occurrence of the beginning of the verse in the context of His saying: "the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-beneficent" — and the function of Lordship is governance (التدبير) while the function of Beneficence is the spreading of mercy — is evidence that what is meant by "addressing Him" (خطابه تعالى) is speaking to Him regarding some of what He has done by way of questioning the reason prompting the act, such as saying: "Why did You do this?" or "Why did You not do that?" just as an actor among us is questioned about his act. Thus, the sentence "they will not be able to address Him" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطاباً) carries the meaning of His saying: "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned" (لا يُسْئَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْئَلُونَ) [Al-Anbiya: 23]. The discussion on the meaning of this verse has already passed.

However, the occurrence of His saying: "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا) after His saying: "they will not be able to address Him," which apparently restricts this lack of ability to the Day of Distinction, added to its occurrence in the context of detailing the reward of the rebels and the God-conscious from Him on the Day of Distinction, suggests that the meaning is that they do not possess the right to address Him regarding what He decrees and does with them by way of objection or intercession. But the angels — who are among those who do not possess the right to address Him — are purified from the blemish of objecting to Him, the Exalted, and He has said regarding them: "honored servants. They do not outstrip Him in speech, and they act by His command" (عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ) [Al-Anbiya: 27]. Likewise is the Spirit (الروح), which is His word (كلمته) and His speech (قوله), and His speech is truth (حق), and He, the Exalted, is the Manifest Truth (الحق المبين), and truth does not oppose truth nor contradict it.

From here it becomes clear that the "address" (الخطاب) which they do not possess is intercession (الشفاعة) and what follows its course of means to escape evil, such as compensation (العدل), trade (البيع), friendship (الخلة), supplication (الدعاء), and petition (السؤال). The Exalted said: "before there comes a day in which there will be no trading, friendship, or intercession" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلا خُلَّةٌ وَلا شَفاعَةٌ) [Al-Baqarah: 254], and said: "nor shall any compensation be accepted from it, nor shall intercession benefit it" (وَلا يُقْبَلُ مِنْها عَدْلٌ وَلا تَنْفَعُها شَفاعَةٌ) [Al-Baqarah: 123], and said: "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave" (يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ) [Hud: 105].

In short, in His saying: "they will not be able to address Him" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطاباً), the pronoun of the subject in "they will not be able" refers to all those gathered for the Day of Distinction — including the angels, the Spirit, humans, and jinn — as is appropriate for the context which narrates the manifestation of Greatness and Majesty, rather than specifically the angels and the Spirit (due to lack of prior mention) or specifically the rebels (as has been suggested) due to the excessive separation. The "address" refers to intercession and what follows its course, as previously mentioned.

His saying: "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا) is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for His saying: "they will not be able." It has been said it is for "they will not speak," but this is remote given the suitability of its being an adverb for what preceded it.

The Spirit (الروح) refers to the creature of the Command (المخلوق الأمري) alluded to in His saying: "Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي) [Al-Isra: 85].

It has been said it refers to the noble ones among the angels; it has been said the guardian angels; it has been said an angel in charge of spirits. There is no evidence for any of these views.

It has also been said it refers to Gabriel; it has been said the spirits of people and their standing with the angels in an array occurs between the two blowings of the Trumpet before they enter the bodies; it has been said the Quran, and the meaning of its standing is the manifestation of its effects on that day in the felicity of the believers in it and the wretchedness of the disbelievers.

These three are refuted by the fact that although each of them is called "Spirit" in His speech, it is always with a restriction, such as His saying: "and breathed into him of My spirit" (وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي) [Al-Hijr: 29], and His saying: "The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down" (نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ) [Ash-Shu'ara: 193], and His saying: "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down" (قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ) [An-Nahl: 102], and His saying: "so We sent to her Our Spirit" (فأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا) [Maryam: 17], and His saying: "And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا) [Ash-Shura: 52]. The Spirit in the verse we are considering is absolute (مطلق). Furthermore, in the last two views, there is an obvious arbitrariness.

"In an array" (صَفًّا) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the Spirit and the angels; it is a verbal noun used in the sense of the active participle — i.e., while they are standing in an array. It may be inferred from the Spirit being contrasted with the angels that the Spirit alone is one array and all the angels are another array.

His saying: "they will not speak" (لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ) is an explanation of His saying: "they will not be able to address Him." The pronoun of the subject refers to the people of the gathering — the Spirit, the angels, humans, and jinn — as the context indicates.

It has been said the pronoun refers to the Spirit and the angels; it has been said to the people. However, the occurrence of "they will not be able" with its aforementioned meaning and "they will not speak" in a single context does not harmonize with either of these two views.

His saying: "except him whom the All-beneficent allows" (إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ) is a substitute (بدل) for the subject pronoun in "they will not speak." It is intended to clarify who among them has the right to speak on that day by the permission of Allah. Thus, the sentence carries the meaning of His saying: "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave" (يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ) [Hud: 105] in its apparent absolute sense.

His saying: "and who says what is right" (وَقالَ صَواباً) — meaning he says a right word unmixed with error, which is the truth (الحق) that falsehood does not enter. The sentence is, in reality, a condition for the permission, as if it were said: "except him whom the All-beneficent allows, and He does not allow except one who says what is right." Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know" (وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ) [Az-Zukhruf: 86].

It has been said: "except him whom [He] allows" etc., is an exception regarding the one spoken about, and the "right" (الصواب) refers to monotheism (التوحيد) and the saying of "There is no god but Allah." The meaning would be: they do not speak on behalf of anyone except on behalf of a person whom the All-beneficent has allowed and who said what is right in the world — i.e., he affirmed the Oneness and bore witness that there is no god but Allah. Thus, the verse would be in the sense of His saying: "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى) [Al-Anbiya: 28].

This is refuted by the fact that the rhetorical focus in this context is concerned with negating the origin of address and speaking on that day from every speaker, not with negating speaking on behalf of everyone while conceding the permissibility of the origin of speaking. Thus, the ones excepted are the speakers who are permitted the origin of speaking, without addressing the one spoken about.

## ( A Discussion on what the Spirit is in the Quran )

The word "Spirit" (الروح) — from which the immediate understanding is the principle of life — is repeated in His speech, the Exalted. He did not restrict it to humans or to humans and animals only, but affirmed it for others as well, as in His saying: "so We sent to her Our Spirit" (فأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا) [Maryam: 17], and His saying: "And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا) [Ash-Shura: 52], among others. Thus, the Spirit has an instance (مصداق) in humans and an instance in others.

That which is suitable to be its definition in His speech is what is in His saying: "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي) [Al-Isra: 85], where He mentioned it absolutely and stated as its definition that it is of His command. He defined His command (أمره) by His saying: "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ) [Ya-Sin: 83]. Thus, He clarified that it is the word of creation (كلمة الإيجاد) which is existence (الوجود) from the perspective of its attribution to Him, the Exalted, and its subsistence by Him, not from the perspective of its attribution to apparent causes and means.

By this consideration, the Messiah (peace be upon him) was counted as a word from Him and a spirit from Him, as He said: "and His Word that He cast toward Mary, and a spirit from Him" (وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ) [An-Nisa: 171], because He bestowed him to Mary (peace be upon her) through other than the usual ways. Close to this in consideration is His saying: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was" (إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ) [Al-Imran: 59].

Although He mentioned it in most of His speech with annexation and restriction, such as His saying: "and breathed into him of My spirit" (وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي) [Al-Hijr: 29], and His saying: "and breathed into him of His spirit" (وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ) [As-Sajdah: 9], and His saying: "so We sent to her Our Spirit" (فأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا) [Maryam: 17], and His saying: "and a spirit from Him" (وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ) [An-Nisa: 171], and His saying: "and We strengthened him with the Holy Spirit" (وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ) [Al-Baqarah: 87], among others — nevertheless, He mentioned it in some of His speech absolutely without restriction, such as His saying: "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command" (تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ) [Al-Qadr: 4]. The apparent meaning of the verse is that it is an independent being and a celestial creation other than the angels. Similar to the verse in a way is His saying: "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose span is fifty thousand years" (تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ) [Al-Ma'arij: 4].

As for the Spirit related to humans, He expressed it with the likes of His saying: "and breathed into him of My spirit" and "and breathed into him of His spirit," using the word "of" (مِنْ) indicating origin (المبدئية) and calling it "breathing" (نفخاً). He expressed the Spirit which He specifically assigned to the believers with the likes of His saying: "and He has strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ) [Al-Mujadilah: 22], using the letter *ba* (ب) indicating causality (السببية) and calling it "strengthening" (تأييداً) and "fortifying." He expressed the Spirit which He specifically assigned to the prophets with the likes of His saying: "and We strengthened him with the Holy Spirit" (وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ) [Al-Baqarah: 87], annexing the Spirit to "Holiness" (القدس) — which is purity and sanctity — and also calling it "strengthening."

By joining these verses to the likes of the verse in Surah al-Qadr, it becomes clear that the relation of the annexed Spirit mentioned in these verses to the absolute Spirit mentioned in Surah al-Qadr is the relation of the emanation (الإفاضة) to the Emanator (المفيض) and the shadow to the possessor of the shadow, by the leave of Allah.

Likewise, the Spirit related to the angels is among the emanations of the Spirit by the leave of Allah. However, He did not express the spirit of the angel with "breathing" or "strengthening" as He did with humans; rather, He called it "Spirit," as in His saying: "so We sent to her Our Spirit," and His saying: "Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down" (قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ) [An-Nahl: 102], and His saying: "The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down" (نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ) [Ash-Shu'ara: 193]. This is because the angels are pure spirits (أرواح محضة) despite their varying ranks in proximity and distance from their Lord. What appears of bodies for them are manifestations (تمثلات), as indicated by His saying: "so We sent to her Our Spirit, and he manifested himself to her as a well-proportioned human" (فأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا) [Maryam: 17]. The discussion on the meaning of manifestation (التمثل) has already passed under that verse. This is in contrast to man, who is created as a composite of a dead body and a living spirit; thus, the expression "breathing" (النفخ) is appropriate for him, as in His saying: "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit" (فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي) [Al-Hijr: 29].

Just as the difference of the Spirit in the creation of the angel and the human necessitated the difference in expression regarding "breathing" and its absence, so too the difference of the Spirit in terms of its effect — which is life — in nobility and baseness necessitated the difference in expression regarding "breathing" and "strengthening," and the counting of the Spirit as having different ranks according to the difference in the effect of life.

Among the Spirit is the Spirit breathed into man; He said: "and breathed into him of My spirit."

Among the Spirit is the Spirit with which the believer is strengthened; He said: "Those—He has written faith in their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ) [Al-Mujadilah: 22]. It is nobler in existence, higher in rank, and more powerful in effect than the general human spirit, as indicated by His saying — which is in the sense of this verse: "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and made for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها) [Al-An'am: 122]. He counted the believer as living, possessed of a light by which he walks — which is the effect of the Spirit — and the disbeliever as dead, even though he possesses a breathed spirit. Thus, the believer has a spirit that the disbeliever does not have, possessed of an effect that is not in him.

From this, it becomes clear that among the ranks of the Spirit is that which is in plants, due to the effect of life within them; this is indicated by the verses containing the giving of life to the earth after its death.

Among the Spirit is the Spirit with which the prophets are strengthened; He said: "and We strengthened him with the Holy Spirit" (وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ) [Al-Baqarah: 87]. The context of the verses indicates that this Spirit is nobler and higher in rank than others found in man.

As for His saying: "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants, that he may warn of the Day of Encounter" (يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ لِيُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلاقِ) [Ghafir: 15], and His saying: "And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit of Our command" (وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا) [Ash-Shura: 52], they accept application to the Spirit of Faith (روح الإيمان) and to the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), and Allah knows best.

Some of what is beneficial in this discussion has already passed under these noble verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الْحَقُ** — **That is the True Day (39).** This is an allusion to the Day of Distinction mentioned in the Surah, described with the aforementioned attributes. It is, in reality, the conclusion of the discourse which turns back to the opening of the Surah. What follows it — namely His saying: "so let anyone who wishes take a resort to his Lord" (فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ مَآباً) etc. — is a further derivation from the previous explanation.

The allusion to it with the distal demonstrative "that" (ذلِكَ) is to indicate the magnitude of its matter. The meaning of its being "true" (حقاً) is its fixedness as a decided decree that will not fail to occur.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ مَآباً** — **so let anyone who wishes take a resort to his Lord (39).** That is, a return to his Lord through which he attains the reward of the God-conscious and is saved from the punishment of the rebels. The sentence, as we pointed out, is a derivation from the preceding announcement of the Day of Distinction, the establishment of proof for it, and its description. The meaning is: since the matter is such, then whoever wishes to return to his Lord, let him return.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ أَنْذَرْناكُمْ عَذاباً قَرِيباً** — **Indeed We have warned you of a punishment near at hand (40)** etc. What is meant by it is the punishment of the Hereafter. Its being "near" is because it is a truth whose coming is beyond doubt, and everything that is coming is near.

Furthermore, the deeds for which man will be rewarded are with him, closer than anything else can be.

His saying: "the day when a man will observe what his hands have sent ahead" (يَوْمَ يَنْظُرُ الْمَرْءُ ما قَدَّمَتْ يَداهُ) — meaning man awaits the reward of his deeds which his hands sent ahead through acquisition. It has been said the meaning is: man looks at the deeds his hands sent ahead due to their being present with him. The Exalted said: "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for whatever evil it has done..." (يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ) [Al-Imran: 30].

His saying: "and the faithless one will say, ‘I wish I were dust!’" (وَيَقُولُ الْكافِرُ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ تُراباً) — meaning he wishes, out of the intensity of that day, that he were dust, devoid of perception and will, so he would not have acted and would not be rewarded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: "and the sky is opened and becomes gates" (وَفُتِحَتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ أَبْواباً), he said: "The gates of the Gardens are opened." Regarding His saying: "and the mountains are set moving and become a mirage" (وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً), he said: "The mountains become like the mirage that shimmers in the wilderness."

In the same source: Regarding His saying: "to reside therein for ages" (لابِثِينَ فِيها أَحْقاباً), he said: "*Al-Ahqab* are years; a *huqb* is a year — and the year consists of three hundred and sixty days — and a day is like a thousand years of what you count."

In *al-Majma‘*, Nafi‘ narrated from Ibn Umar who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'No one who enters the Fire shall emerge from it until he remains therein for ages (*ahqaba*) — and a *huqb* is sixty-odd years, and a year is three hundred and sixty days — each day is like a thousand years of what you count — so let no one rely on emerging from the Fire.'"

**I say:** This narration was also cited in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, and in it "eighty" appears instead of "sixty," and the wording of its end is: "Ibn Umar said: 'So let no one rely...'" etc. Another narration was also cited from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that a *huqb* is forty years.

In the same source, al-Ayyashi narrated with his chain from Humran who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about this verse, and he said: 'This is regarding those who emerge from the Fire.'" A similar narration was reported from al-Ahwal.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: "Indeed for the God-conscious there is a triumph" (إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ مَفازاً), he said: "They triumph." Regarding His saying: "and buxom maidens of equal age" (وَكَواعِبَ أَتْراباً), he said: "Maidens and those of equal age for the people of Paradise." In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said regarding His saying: "Indeed for the God-conscious there is a triumph": "It refers to the honors (الكرامات)." "and buxom maidens of equal age": "meaning young women with swelling breasts."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh in *al-Azamah*, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The Spirit is an army from the armies of Allah — they are not angels; they have heads, hands, and feet." Then he recited: "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا). He said: "These are an army and those are an army."

**I say:** The narration has already passed under the verses containing the Spirit from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that the Spirit is a creation greater than Gabriel and Michael. The narration also passed from Ali (peace be upon him) that the Spirit is other than the angels — and he (peace be upon him) argued for this using His saying, the Exalted: "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" (يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ) [An-Nahl: 2].

Yes, in the narration of al-Qummi from Humran, it is an angel greater than Gabriel and Michael, and he was with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he is with the Imams (peace be upon them). Perhaps what is meant by "angel" is any celestial being, or it is from the misconception of some narrators in reporting the meaning. There is no evidence for the restriction of the celestial beings of the Command (الموجودات الأمرية السماوية) to the angels; rather, the evidence is to the contrary, as inferred from His saying, the Exalted, to Iblis when he refused to prostrate to Adam while all the angels had prostrated: "O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating to that which I have created with My two hands? Were you arrogant, or were you among the exalted ones?" (يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ) [Sad: 75]. This was already alluded to in the commentary of that verse.

In *Usul al-Kafi*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from Abu al-Hasan al-Madi (peace be upon him), he said: "I said: 'On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in an array, they will not speak...' (the verse). He said: 'By Allah, we are the ones permitted for them on the Day of Resurrection — and the ones who say what is right.' I said: 'What do you say when you speak?' He said: 'We glorify our Lord, send blessings upon our Prophet — and intercede for our followers (شيعتنا), and our Lord does not reject us.'" (The tradition).

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Majma‘* from al-Ayyashi, raised to Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him).

The narration is of the type of mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق); for there are other intercessors from the angels, the prophets, and the believers who are permitted to speak, and there are witnesses (شهداء) from the nations who are permitted to speak, according to what the Quran and the traditions explicitly state.
